data.title: President Trump's QE Plans Could Boost Housing And The Stock Market
data.description:
As we all know, President Trump has said that Fed Chair Jerome Powell is "too late" when it comes to interest rate cuts, and he has even talked about firing him. Jerome Powell's term ends in May, and President Trump is expected to appoint a new Fed Chair soon, but it seems that he can't wait that long to start lowering rates as well as come up with stimulus for the U.S. economy. There's a good reason for President Trump to want to boost the economy as well as the stock market as soon as possible, and that is because the midterm elections are coming up later this year.

Midterm elections are typically not favorable for the party that holds the White House, and with the job market showing signs of deterioration over the past few months, things could get worse in the coming months if nothing is done to stimulate the economy soon. In addition, in the recent high-profile New York City Mayor race, a socialist Democrat who spoke to voters about affordability came out on top in the election. All of this suggests that President Trump needs to take action since the Federal Reserve has not been able to significantly reduce mortgage rates, and he also needs to work to make life more affordable for voters.

The latest news is that Jerome Powell is being criminally investigated by the Department of Justice for his testimony regarding renovations at the historic Federal Reserve building. I view this as a concern because it is unprecedented, and it could lead to volatility in the financial markets. However, the market seems to be shrugging off any concerns for now, which shows resilience, but this still could have consequences for the markets later on. This move gives the potential appearance that the Fed will be less independent under Trump and that Jerome Powell's replacement could be viewed as President Trump's right-hand man at the Fed. The bond market could revolt against this if it decides that inflation is being swept aside by the Fed in the future.

Let's take a look at some challenges and at how President Trump is taking action to stimulate the economy and lower the cost of living, because if his plans work, it might be just what is needed to stabilize the job market, boost growth, and even improve consumer confidence before the all-important midterm elections. Let's start with some of the economic and stock market challenges first and then go into what Trump is doing to create his own version of QE and other stimulus:

Trump's QE Plan Could Be the Catalyst This Market Needs

The S&P 500 Index (SPY) is near record highs, which is right around the 7,000 level. This is a round number, and it seems to be a resistance level since it traded close to this level in October and December, as well as right now. If you look at the chart below, you can see that this index appears to be having difficulty in making a decisive breakthrough past the 7,000 level. If a breakout past this level keeps getting rejected, it will make a multiple top pattern more clear on the chart, and that could lead to a breakdown in this index rather than a breakthrough. However, if President Trump continues to roll out ideas that are intended to boost the economy, it could be just what is needed to create a decisive upward move for stocks.

Consumer Confidence is Extremely Weak

The Consumer Confidence Index has been generally trending lower for the past few years, and it is now approaching a level not seen since the lows of the pandemic. I view this as a warning sign for the economy and the stock market, since sustained and significant declines in this index are typical during times of recession. This can be seen on the chart below during the Great Financial Crisis and the pandemic. The Consumer Confidence Index dropped by 3.8 points in December amid apparent concerns over the job market and household income.

Housing Unaffordable for Many Americans

In December I wrote an article suggesting that President Trump plans to make housing more affordable in 2026 and that investors should consider buying beaten-down stocks that are related to housing. This included stocks like Whirlpool (WHR), RE/MAX (RMAX), Lennar (LEN), Home Depot (HD), and even RH (RH). All of these stocks are up significantly in the past couple of weeks, but there could be more gains ahead if President Trump unleashes a sustained rebound in the housing market, which has been frozen for years. But, as shown in the chart below, housing prices are at bubble-like levels, and while President Trump's recent initiatives can ease some of the affordability issues, more needs to be done to make a dent in the high price of home ownership.

Trump's QE Plan

QE, or Quantitative Easing, is a monetary policy that is typically undertaken by central banks in order to stimulate the economy. It typically works by having the central bank buy financial assets such as government bonds in order to inject money into the economy and lower interest rates. QE is typically used when traditional interest rate cuts are not enough to get the economy moving.

On January 8th, President Trump announced that he would ask his representatives to buy $200 billion worth of mortgage bonds in an attempt to lower mortgage rates. Not all the details are clear on this plan yet, but the market moved on this news, and mortgage rates dropped immediately from about 6.2% down to around 6%. It remains to be seen as to whether this reduction in mortgage rates will be sustained and if the mortgage bond buying plan will even happen.

Boosting Consumer Sentiment, Economy

There doesn't seem to be any quick fix or singular solution to boosting consumer confidence or in making housing affordable again, so it seems that President Trump is taking a multi-pronged approach. One of the biggest potential positives could be his recent initiatives in Venezuela, which used to be a country that produced much more oil. President Trump is pushing for American oil companies to go back into Venezuela and get production back up, and he has a stated goal to push the price of oil down to around $50 per barrel. Lower oil prices could reduce inflation in many areas, and that could ease bond market concerns over inflation and allow for more interest rate cuts.

President Trump recently announced a proposal to ban institutional investors from buying single-family homes. I think this is a great idea that will resonate with many voters and possibly increase the amount of homes that are on the market for families to buy instead of corporations. President Trump is planning to announce more details on this and other potential housing affordability plans when he speaks at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, which is scheduled to happen later this month.

Some of the other initiatives by President Trump to boost the economy, housing, and consumer confidence include a proposal for 50-year mortgages and a 10% cap on the interest rate charged by credit card companies. I don't view these proposals as being a good solution because a 50-year mortgage will also carry a higher interest rate, thereby offsetting some of the benefits of a lower (for longer) payment.

While the 10% cap on credit card interest rates could be very popular with voters in the short term, I believe this could backfire and actually cause an economic contraction because the banks could simply decide not to maintain credit card accounts with lower-quality borrowers without a commensurate reward for the risk. If banks respond by closing accounts, this could cut off a key source of consumer spending, which is critical to the U.S. economy.

Risk Factors to Consider

While I am not investing in the major market indexes due to high concentration risks and what I see as overvaluation, I do see value in beaten-down housing-related stocks, so I am buying some stocks in this sector. However, it remains to be seen if many of President Trump's affordability plans will even happen and if they will be successful in the long term. While the Trump "QE" plan to lower mortgage rates did create an immediate decline in mortgage rates, this could just be a short-term move that is unsustainable.

I am concerned about a potential recession that could develop if consumer confidence and the job market do not stabilize soon. If a recession ensues, the stock market could plunge from record high levels, and the housing stocks I have been buying will also likely see major declines. With unconventional and possibly untested ideas, there is also a chance for policy errors, which I believe could backfire, such as trying to cap credit card interest rates.

In Summary

The housing market has been frozen for a few years due to the big increase in mortgage rates that has occurred since the pandemic. Long-term interest rates have not been very responsive to recent Fed rate cuts, and this has kept mortgage rates elevated. However, President Trump appears prepared to do what it takes to reduce mortgage rates by potentially creating his own QE, as well as other affordability initiatives. If he succeeds in unfreezing the housing market, he could unleash a powerful turnaround in this beaten-down sector and provide the economy with a major boost.

data.title: Deutsche Bank: From Rerating To Proof-Of-Execution
data.description:
Introduction

Deutsche Bank Aktiengesellschaft's (DB) shares trading above book value for the first time since 2008 marks a genuine inflection point, but not for the reason equity bulls might hope. This is not the start of a new growth phase but rather the formal end of the recovery trade. After more than a decade in which investors priced in litigation risk, capital fragility, and latent balance-sheet problems, the market is now prepared to accept Deutsche Bank’s reported equity at face value. That trust reset is relevant, but what follows from it is more constrained.

For years, Deutsche Bank traded at a discount because survival itself was questioned. Legacy legal settlements, repeated restructurings, and the corrosive effect of negative interest rates in Europe left earnings volatile and confidence fragile. When the stock briefly touched 0.2x book during the early stages of the pandemic, the market was effectively pricing in a scenario where equity could be impaired again. That period is now over, as shares have risen above the bank’s most recently reported book value. That milestone reflects the cumulative effect of resolved legal disputes, exits from loss-making activities, and a more stable earnings base.

Why the Tier 2 deal matters more than the share price

The bank’s recent activity in the credit markets deserves at least as much attention as the equity milestone. In January, Deutsche Bank issued a €1 billion euro-denominated Tier 2 bond with a maturity of just over 15 years and a first call after roughly 10 years. GlobalCapital described the deal as “landmark”, highlighting that it was among the longest-dated euro Tier 2 instruments issued by a bank since 2020 and that it benefited from strong investor demand for duration in regulatory capital. This was not forced funding. It was opportunistic balance-sheet management at a moment when market access was favorable.

The signal embedded in that transaction is subtle but important. Credit investors were willing to underwrite long-duration subordinated capital because they no longer see Deutsche Bank as a restructuring story with binary risk. Tier 2 buyers are effectively making a judgment about the bank’s ability to remain a going concern well beyond the next cycle. Extending the maturity profile of regulatory capital reduces refinancing risk and smooths the capital stack, something that would have been far harder to achieve when confidence was fragile. In that sense, the deal reinforces the downside protection of the equity.

Capital return becomes the only equity lever

For equity holders, the relevance of the Tier 2 issuance lies in how it interacts with capital return. Deutsche Bank has made clear that it wants to move into a phase of predictable distributions. Management has guided to a return on tangible equity above 10% for 2025 and reiterated a medium-term ambition of more than 13% by 2028. Alongside that, the bank has communicated a payout ratio target of around 60% for the period from 2026 onward, assuming capital levels remain comfortably above regulatory requirements.

At sub-book valuations, buybacks are mechanically attractive. Repurchasing equity below its accounting value boosts book value per share and offers clear accretion even if returns are mediocre. At book value, that mechanical appeal fades. Buybacks still make sense if the bank can earn above its cost of equity, but they no longer generate automatic value. From here, capital return only works if it is durable and underpinned by stable earnings. The equity story shifts from “Can Deutsche Bank return capital?” to “Can it keep doing so through the cycle?”

Structural ROE limits keep the multiple anchored

This is where structural limits assert themselves. Deutsche Bank’s business mix remains capital-intensive, particularly due to its investment banking activities. The investment bank has performed well in recent years, especially in fixed income trading, and has benefited from volatile but active markets. Those revenues can meaningfully lift profits in good periods, but they also raise the volatility of returns and the cost of equity investors apply.

The Financial Times explicitly notes this gap when comparing Deutsche Bank to peers. While analysts expect the bank to hit its 10% return target for 2025, its longer-term ambition of around 13% still lags some European rivals aiming for materially higher returns. A bank earning returns roughly in line with its cost of equity tends to trade around book value. To justify a sustained premium, it needs to demonstrate through-cycle returns that clearly clear that hurdle. In the European regulatory environment, with its emphasis on capital buffers and supervisory caution, that is difficult for an investment-bank-heavy model to achieve.

At roughly €33–34 per share, Deutsche Bank trades close to 1.0x reported and tangible book, implying a market capitalization of about €65 billion. On management’s >10% RoTE target for 2025, the implied earnings yield sits in the high single digits, broadly in line with a 10–11% cost of equity for a European bank with a sizable investment banking arm. Even if the bank reaches its medium-term ambition of ~13% RoTE, a plausible valuation range remains around 1.0–1.2x tangible book, suggesting limited upside from multiple expansion. The share price can grind higher through earnings and distributions, but a meaningful rerating would require returns to exceed the cost of equity by a wider and more durable margin than the current business mix is likely to deliver.

Conclusion

Put together, the equity rerating and the Tier 2 issuance describe a bank that has moved from turnaround to maintenance. The market is no longer debating survival. It is debating consistency. The rerating reflects reduced perceived risk, not the emergence of a new growth engine.

For investors, that means a different risk-reward profile. Deutsche Bank is now priced for execution. Any earnings wobble, regulatory intervention, or capital markets slowdown will feed through quickly because there is little valuation cushion left. Conversely, upside from here depends on management delivering exactly what it has promised: steady returns, tight cost control, and predictable capital distributions, year after year. The recovery phase is over. The maintenance phase has begun.

data.title: World Stocks End 2025 Strong With December Gain
data.description:
As December 2025 came to a close, markets reflected a mixed performance across asset classes. World Stocks led the way, posting a +2.57% return for the month and solidifying their position as 2025’s top performer with an impressive +32.60% gain.

Managed futures finished strong with a +1.15% return in December, marking its fifth consecutive positive month. Despite this late-year rally, Managed Futures ended 2025 nearly flat at -0.19%.

Hedge funds posted a +0.54% gain for December, bringing their year-to-date return to +8.28%. U.S. stocks ended quietly with a +0.08% monthly return, finishing 2025 up +17.71%.

Several asset classes retreated in December. U.S. real estate declined -2.21%, reducing its annual gain to +3.42%. Commodities fell -0.90%, ending the year up +5.92%, while bonds dipped -0.30%, closing 2025 with a +7.10% return.

Cash continued its steady performance with a +0.30% December gain, accumulating a +4.22% return for 2025. The year concluded with all major asset classes except managed futures posting YTD positive returns, led by world stocks’ substantial outperformance.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Sources: Managed Futures = SocGen CTA Index

Cash = US T-Bill 13 week coupon equivalent annual rate/12, with YTD the sum of each month’s value

Bonds = Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND)

Hedge Funds = IQ Hedge Multi-Strategy Tracker ETF (QAI)

Commodities = iShares S&P GSCI Commodity-Indexed Trust ETF (GSG)

Real Estate = iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF (IYR)

World Stocks = iShares MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. ETF (ACWX)

US Stocks = SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY)

All ETF performance data from Y Charts

Disclaimer

The performance data displayed herein is compiled from various sources, including BarclayHedge, and reports directly from the advisors. These performance figures should not be relied on independent of the individual advisor's disclosure document, which has important information regarding the method of calculation used, whether or not the performance includes proprietary results, and other important footnotes on the advisor's track record.

The programs listed here are a subset of the full list of programs able to be accessed by subscribing to the database and reflect programs we currently work with and/or are more familiar with.

Benchmark index performance is for the constituents of that index only, and does not represent the entire universe of possible investments within that asset class. And further, that there can be limitations and biases to indices such as survivorship, self reporting, and instant history. Individuals cannot invest in the index itself, and actual rates of return may be significantly different and more volatile than those of the index.

Managed futures accounts can subject to substantial charges for management and advisory fees. The numbers within this website include all such fees, but it may be necessary for those accounts that are subject to these charges to make substantial trading profits in the future to avoid depletion or exhaustion of their assets.

Investors interested in investing with a managed futures program (excepting those programs which are offered exclusively to qualified eligible persons as that term is defined by CFTC regulation 4.7) will be required to receive and sign off on a disclosure document in compliance with certain CFT rules The disclosure documents contains a complete description of the principal risk factors and each fee to be charged to your account by the CTA, as well as the composite performance of accounts under the CTA's management over at least the most recent five years. Investor interested in investing in any of the programs on this website are urged to carefully read these disclosure documents, including, but not limited to the performance information, before investing in any such programs.

Those investors who are qualified eligible persons as that term is defined by CFTC regulation 4.7 and interested in investing in a program exempt from having to provide a disclosure document and considered by the regulations to be sophisticated enough to understand the risks and be able to interpret the accuracy and completeness of any performance information on their own.

RCM receives a portion of the commodity brokerage commissions you pay in connection with your futures trading and/or a portion of the interest income (if any) earned on an account's assets. The listed manager may also pay RCM a portion of the fees they receive from accounts introduced to them by RCM.

Limitations on RCM Quintile + Star Rankings

The Quintile Rankings and RCM Star Rankings shown here are provided for informational purposes only. RCM does not guarantee the accuracy, timeliness or completeness of this information. The ranking methodology is proprietary and the results have not been audited or verified by an independent third party. Some CTAs may employ trading programs or strategies that are riskier than others. CTAs may manage customer accounts differently than their model results shown or make different trades in actual customer accounts versus their own accounts. Different CTAs are subject to different market conditions and risks that can significantly impact actual results. RCM and its affiliates receive compensation from some of the rated CTAs. Investors should perform their own due diligence before investing with any CTA. This ranking information should not be the sole basis for any investment decision.

See the full terms of use and risk disclaimer here.

Original Post

data.title: Aquestive Therapeutics: Anaphylm Will Probably Survive This Regulatory Hurdle
data.description:
Back in October 2025, I shared with my followers this personal story and investment analysis on Aquestive Therapeutics (AQST). Based upon the information that I had then, I rated the stock a cautious BUY based upon my guarded optimism that its leading asset, Anaphylm, a sublingual epinephrine film, would likely be approved by the FDA to treat severe allergies.

On January 9, 2026, the company disclosed through this Form 8-K that the FDA had notified the company it had "identified deficiencies in the NDA that preclude discussion of labeling and post-marketing commitments [of Anaphylm] at this time".

This development has caused Mr. Market to push the company's stock price down by 40% or so. Practically speaking, Mr. Market now assumes that the probability for an FDA approval and commercial success is reduced by a similar magnitude.

Now let's try to put this development into regulatory context and reassess what has actually changed—and what has not—in order for us to arrive at an educated speculation as to whether Anaphylm will jump through this regulatory hurdle and roll into the commercial market.

What This Update Clearly Means

At the risk of stating the obvious, this is a negative development that increases the probability of a delay and raises the possibility of a Complete Response Letter from the FDA (read: practically a rejection) if the issues are not resolved. It means the review is no longer a smooth glide path.

However, this development is not a rejection, at least not a rejection yet. That is, the FDA's review of Anaphylm remains ongoing and no final decision has been made.

What the FDA has said is that, in its current form, something in the Anaphylm new drug application prevents the agency from moving into the final phase of the review process-namely, negotiating labeling and post-marketing commitments.

In plain English, the FDA is telling the company, "We are not yet at a point where this application is approvable unless and until we clear this [unspecified] hurdle."

What This Update Does Not Say

But it is not, by itself, a verdict on the efficacy or safety of Anaphylm itself. The press release does not say the drug is ineffective, or the drug is unsafe, or the clinical data are inadequate. Historically, when the FDA reaches negative conclusions about the efficacy or safety of a drug candidate, the relevant company's disclosure usually has to reflect that directly. The language used here is procedural in essence.

In regulatory practice, communications stating that deficiencies "preclude labeling [further] discussions" often arise when the FDA believes there are unresolved application-blocking issues. Such issues frequently [although not exclusively] are related to manufacturing quality, product stability, packaging, etc.-rather than a fundamental failure or deficiency of the therapeutic agent itself.

That does not make the problem an immaterial challenge. But at least at this point in time, it does not appear to point to a failure of the therapeutic agent itself, which would be much more difficult to overcome.

Why This Surfaced So Late in the Review

A lot of investors hope the FDA review will be a quiet process that ends with a simple yes or no. This is especially true in the case of Anaphylm, given that the FDA had decided to forego the AdCom procedure earlier.

In practice, late-cycle review is often when internal FDA discipline teams consolidate their findings and determine whether an application is approvable. Actually, it is not uncommon for the agency to formally communicate that material deficiencies remain in its current form in the late stage of the review. Sometimes those issues are resolved quickly. Sometimes they lead to a formal "not approvable yet" decision.

Why the Company Disclosed This Now

Note that yours truly does not provide any legal or investment advice here to anyone here. From the perspective of securities law compliance, once a company receives a formal FDA communication stating that approval-blocking deficiencies exist for their NDA, it is risky for the company to take a legal position that the existence of that fact alone is immaterial, i.e., not required to disclose to the stock market.

The theoretical reason is that a "reasonable investor"-that legal fiction we lawyers are so fond of invoking-would likely view the information, standing alone, cryptic and preliminary as it may be, as important to how the market should value the company. The practical reason is even simpler: failing to disclose receipt of the FDA letter promptly would likely hand my zealous brethren in the securities class-action plaintiff bar the ground to sustain a lawsuit, creating enough pressure to force the company to settle it.

What Changed in the Investment Thesis

Three things have changed: first, timeline risk has increased. Approval on the original target date is now less likely than it was before this update.

Second, regulatory execution risk is now explicit, although for the reason stated above, I am personally not convinced that the risk is of such a nature or magnitude that it justifies a 40% decline in its stock price.

Third, cash runway matters more. The company's emphasis on approximately $120 million in cash as of December 31, 2025, signals that the company is to a large extent prepared financially for the possibility of an extended regulatory process. Its latest Form 10-Q indicates that for the 9 months ending on Sept 30, 2025, the company spent approximately $44 million net on operating activities (line 15, the Condensed Statement of Cash Flow), i.e., about $5 million each month. At this rate, the existing cash shall last about two years.

Bottom Line

Late-cycle FDA communications raising unresolved deficiencies are not rare in drug development, particularly for products involving novel delivery systems or emergency-use profiles. Some lead to delayed approvals. Some lead to formal non-approval letters followed by eventual resubmission and success. Some do fail.

The dispositive issue now becomes whether the issues raised by the FDA are resolvable within a reasonable time and cost framework?

Given Aquestive's rich institutional experiences and expertise in sublingual film manufacturing, packaging, and delivery, and the efficacy and safety profiles of Anaphylm, I am cautiously optimistic that the company will overcome this hurdle.

So, on balance, I am not abandoning the view that Anaphylm can ultimately be approved. However, we bulls do need to understand that a position in Aquestive now requires more risk tolerance and patience for delays and even setbacks. If and when this regulatory hurdle is behind us, the stock shall return to its previous level of around $6 and, depending on the commercial performance of Anaphylm, climb up to $8-$15 in the long term.

To that end, I have decided to increase my position in the company. However, at the risk of stating the obvious, one shall not allocate proportionately too much capital to any stock with similar binary regulatory outcomes, and only a basket of carefully selected biotech stocks has the potential to beat the market.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Beaten Down AI Player
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META)(META:CA) got overheated over the summer when the stock surged to $800 on AI excitement. The social media leader hasn't necessarily executed on AI as expected, but the stock is now much cheaper at $650 due to the strong legacy business. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish after this consolidation period at lower prices with signs Meta is finally pulling back from the wild spending on the Metaverse.

Struggling AI Position

Meta has faced a lot of concerns; the company is overspending on AI while the team is underdelivering. The social media giant benefits from using AI to make their social media networks more efficient and deliver higher-quality ads, but the company has really struggled to gain any real traction outside the corporate walls.

The company is expected to launch a new LLM model dubbed Avocado along with an AI image model named Mango in the next few months after acquiring Scale AI and talent from OpenAI. According to data from Similarweb, Meta has fallen far behind Google Gemini and ChatGPT from OpenAI in generative AI traffic. Even startups like Grok, Perplexity, and Claude from Anthropic have more successful AI businesses.

A couple of weeks back, Meta was rumored to plan 30% cuts to the Reality Labs division. The company has been losing $17+ billion a year on building out the Metaverse concept and AR/VR glasses.

The business has lost over $73 billion already and will approach $95 billion by the end of 2026 without any cuts. Considering the Reality Labs business has limited revenues, a 30% cut in expenses goes directly to the bottom line.

The division could cut $5 billion in expenses and reduce the annual losses to $12 billion. At the same time, Meta is seeing surging demand in the smart glasses sold via Ray-Ban and Oakley.

The Ray-Ban Display costs $799, and the company has reportedly sold 15,000 units. The company pushed EssilorLuxottica (ESLOF) to increase capacity to 10 million units for 2026 of the $300 glasses, so it's not clear where production stands for the nearly $800 version.

Meta has delayed plans to roll out the Meta glasses internationally due to huge demand in the U.S., so the division could add a huge injection of revenues to further cut the cash burn from Reality Labs. The division can add $5.50 to EPS via ultimately eliminating the losses.

Naturally, the money saved will flow into the AI spending where Meta is already seeing a more direct benefit. The company is already seeing $60 billion worth of ads flow through the AI-powered ad tools.

The below chart shows Meta's total expenses with capex included versus depreciation charges. While total expenses are up in the last few years, the social media company isn't spending wildly higher amounts from this perspective.

A big focus on the Q4 earnings report coming up at the end of January is the planned level of capex spending in 2026. On the Q3'25 earnings call, the CFO outlined a scenario where Meta would boost capital expenditures dollar growth by a significant amount in 2026 as follows:

As a result, our current expectation is that CapEx dollar growth will be notably larger in 2026 than 2025. We also anticipate total expenses will grow at a significantly faster percentage rate in 2026 than 2025, with growth primarily driven by infrastructure costs, including incremental cloud expenses and depreciation.

Meta's capex guide implies a $30+ billion YoY increase in 2026 capex from the $71 billion midpoint for 2025. In essence, the company gave a guide to 2026 capex at least hitting $100 billion, with the language suggesting something closer to the $105 to $110 billion range.

At the same time, Meta mentioned higher infrastructure costs from cloud expenses. The company has made several large deals with cloud providers that aren't even part of capex.

Still A Profit Machine

The capex amounts quickly get large on any increases from the above levels. Meta is only forecast to reach 2026 revenues of $235 billion and up to $272 billion in 2027 on revenue growth of 16%.

Meta quickly absorbs this capex spending when the company adds $72 billion in additional revenues over the next 2-year period. The company just can't sustain adding higher levels of capex than revenue growth, as the free cash flow will disappear with operating margins in the 40% range.

Despite all of the wild spending, Meta is forecast to hit a 2026 EPS of $30.42. As mentioned above, the Reality Labs division cost the company about $5.50 in EPS, and an elimination of those losses pushes the EPS to ~$36.

The stock trades at just 21.5x 2026 EPS targets and 18x the adjusted EPS. Meta is very appealing down here around $650 with so many levers to pull to boost EPS via just cutting costs, including some of the accelerated AI spending.

Meta either becomes a leader in AI, or the company could quickly pivot from heavy internal capex spending and start using external generative AI models. Just as in Reality Labs, Meta now appears to have a successful smart glasses product, and the company can quickly cut costs to focus on the success story.

The biggest risk is Meta overspending on AI infrastructure with no going-forward benefit. The consensus analyst EPS estimates could be far too high in a scenario where the AI spending doesn't boost revenues, leading to the company reporting lower earnings with costs soaring without incremental revenues.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Meta is one of the more appealing mega-cap tech stocks. The company hasn't fully turned the massive AI spending into top notch AI products, but the stock is now exceptionally cheap, and Meta has the potential to turn around the AI models to boost the business.

Investors should use the ongoing weakness in the stock to play the AI story at a discount.

data.title: Restaurant Brands International: Defying Industry Weakness With Accelerating Sales
data.description:
So far, the US stock markets are holding on fairly firm ground in 2026 as investors' bullishness continues. Underneath the surface, however, we should be prepared for a rotation out of expensive, momentum-driven tech stocks and into underappreciated categories that have recently underperformed.

The restaurant sector, in my view, is ripe for a turnaround. Many investors have ditched the space on account of slowing comp sales but are ignoring a number of stronger-performing names in the group. Restaurant Brands International (QSR), the parent of Tim Hortons and Burger King, is among the stocks that are overdue for a rally. Up ~10% in choppy trading over the past year, RBI has actually achieved meaningful comp sales acceleration in a tough environment. While it has its challenges, the stock is worth buying on its recent dip from the mid-$70s.

I'm initiating Restaurant Brands at a buy rating. Over the last quarter, it has become increasingly evident that consumers are pulling away from fast casual but sticking with fast food names, and RBI may be a good way to capture that shift.

For overall context on the company, Restaurant Brands is composed of six reporting divisions. Its principal four brands are Tim Hortons, Burger King, Popeyes Louisiana Kitchen, and Firehouse Subs. A separate international division reports the company's revenue in these brands outside the U.S. and Canada. And a sixth division, Restaurant Holdings, represents Burger King, Popeyes, and Firehouse Subs locations reacquired from franchisees over the past two years.

Despite a reasonable valuation and a healthy dividend, RBI is experiencing improved performance, particularly in its heaviest-hitting brands, Tim Hortons and Burger King domestically, which we'll focus on next.

Tim Hortons is Showcasing Accelerating Comp Sales

With the only possible exception of Dunkin' Donuts, there are probably few global brands that are more synonymous with donuts and coffee than Tim Hortons. What draws us to RBI in particular is that despite a slowdown in global consumer spending and weaker comps at many restaurant chains, Tim Hortons is showcasing surprising strength.

In Q3, Tim Hortons achieved 4.8% comp sales growth systemwide. As a reminder, comp sales measures the revenue growth from existing store locations, excluding the contribution of newly opened or newly closed locations. Comps accelerated versus 2.7% growth for the YTD period and are up 2x versus 2.4% in the year-ago Q3.

This brand is most prominent in its home market of Canada, and here, management notes that Tim Hortons is outperforming the Canadian quick-service restaurant industry by 3 points in comps. There's likely an element of Tim Hortons being a cheap, quick comfort in an otherwise tough economy here. Management notes as well that grab-and-go breakfast menu items, like the recently launched Loaded Croissant, have been strong performance drivers.

But equally driving success in Tim Hortons' focus on boosting traffic during different dayparts, i.e., serving customers beyond just the mornings. The company notes that it has launched $8.99 dinner deals with great success. Again, it appears here that consumers are resonating with the company's value offerings, similar to what we're observing from peers like McDonald's.

Though Tim Hortons is only a fifth of worldwide systemwide sales, it represented 46% of Q3 revenue (a greater portion of these locations are company-owned, versus the franchises where overseas locations are mostly third-party owned), so Tim Hortons' brand momentum is of special importance to the company.

BK and International Are Seeing Sales Improvements as Well

But RBI's recent success goes beyond just its largest second. Its second most critical brand, Burger King, is showing signs of a revival as well.

As shown in the chart below, BK also achieved healthy 3.1% comp sales growth worldwide, with 3.2% growth in the U.S. Again, this showed acceleration versus 1.2% growth in the YTD period.

Burger King has been a big turnaround story for RBI. Several years ago, the company launched its "reclaim the flame" initiative, which involved investing in restaurant interiors and technology and experimenting with its menu. We note as well that the company has closed a number of underperforming locations strategically, which has led to -1.1% net location growth. Still, systemwide sales grew, while RBI's direct revenue from Burger King grew 7% y/y to $387 million.

In my childhood, I thought of the "Big 3" fast food burger chains as McDonald's (MCD), Burger King, and Wendy's (WEN) - in that mental order. Burger King's Q3 US comps outperformed McDonald's at 2.4% U.S. growth:

And it also outperformed Wendy's by a wide mile, as Wendy's declined -4.7%:

And if you're inclined to think deeper promotions are part of the reason for the sales recovery, think again: BK's operating margins have also improved. Adjusted operating income growth of 10% y/y outpaced revenue growth, representing a segment margin of 31.8% that improved 90bps y/y.

And across brands, the international segment saw the fastest comps growth at 6.5% y/y, speeding up versus the YTD growth of 4.5%.

Per CEO Joshua Kobza's remarks on the Q3 earnings call on the drivers of international success:

Our same-store sales outperformed the industry in several key markets. including France, the U.K., Spain and Germany. In France, performance strengthened with the successful launch of our Baby burger boxes in July, a shareable snacking platform that's been a big hit with our guests.

In September, we expanded our chef collaboration platform to the U.K. with the launch of the Gordon Ramsay Wagyu burger made with 100% British Wagyu beef, which drove strong engagement and sales. This quarter, we also leveraged our global scale with a cross-market promotion of Naruto, the popular anime series, which performed well across countries like Germany, Brazil and China."

Cheap Valuation Amid Consistent Growth Profile

Now we come to the crux of my buy thesis: despite the strength of RBI's recent results, which are clearly well above peer comp sales trends, RBI continues to trade at quite modest levels.

The company's long-term growth algorithm calls for 3.0% comp sales growth annually, which the company's consolidated 4.2% growth in Q3 exceeded - again, despite a tough macro. It's also expecting 8%+ annual operating income appreciation.

For FY26, Wall Street is expecting the company to deliver 4% sales growth and $4.02 in pro forma EPS (+9% y/y).

So at current share prices near $69, RBI is trading at just a 17.2x forward P/E, which is a touch below the S&P 500. It's not a value stock, but considering RBI's market share gains (as evidenced by comps well outpacing peers), I'm more than happy to pay a high teens earnings multiple for quality and consistency.

Q4 Look-Ahead and Risks

RBI is due to report Q4 results in late January. To me, the items to watch out for are comp sales trends. We're hoping for comps to at least remain at the same pace as in Q3 and for the gap to other fast food peers to widen. We think there's opportunity for share grab as well as consumers rotate away from fast casual (the so-called "slop bowl" chains like Chipotle (CMG) and CAVA (CAVA)). Also, we're hopeful for an update about the company's location expansion plans for calendar 2026, especially given the company's recent commentary about wanting to achieve meaningful location growth in Tim Hortons while it's building on recent strength.

Of course, there are risks that we need to be mindful of here. The macro remains a big concern, and while it appears that consumers have gravitated toward fast food companies' value offerings recently, this trend could shift abruptly. Also, there are secular risks at hand: healthier eating is more in than ever, which could pressure the entire fast food category in the long haul.

The other risk is RBI's debt mound. Especially after investing in the overhaul of Burger King, the company is sitting at a materially high leverage ratio. Net debt sits at $2.9 billion, which is a trailing leverage ratio of 4.4x. Commercial banks typically consider 3x as the normal threshold for a highly leveraged business.

That said, we do note that the company's comps growth is translating into meaningful free cash flow expansion. YTD FCF of $996 million is up 11% y/y, so the company has the potential to deleverage fairly quickly.

Key Takeaways

All in all, the restaurant sector appears due for a rebound, especially for the stronger-performing names like RBI in the group. The stock's current ~3.5% yield also offers extra incentive for patience. Stay long here and bank on a rebound.

data.title: Rates Spark: Dutch Pensions Transition And A Fed Attack
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist; Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist; and Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

The second-largest Dutch pension fund published new details about their hedging strategy, challenging speculative positions for steeper curves. Expect more volatility from the very long end. We also reflect on the fallout from the remarkable attack on the Federal Reserve over the weekend, and how it might pan out ahead.

Reflections on Federal Reserve interference and the markets

Note that Chair Powell is not being hauled into court just yet. There is a grand jury proceeding to get through first. This is the precedent to the case getting to court. Note that the grand jury could also decide that there is no case to answer, and then that would be the end of the story; no court case.

Typically, though, the grand jury has quite a low bar to surpass to get a case to an actual court, but it is still a bar that needs to be surpassed. The grand jury proceeding could take weeks, and if we get through that, an actual case could take months. This will run and run, and Chair Powell, it seems, is not for caving.

This, in part, is why the market reaction has been very muted. The other part-rationale, as summarised here, reflects the basic mathematics that we are dealing with. If Chair Powell were removed, and Lisa Cook were removed, and we got one retirement morphed into a 'MAGA dove' in 2026, we would then have a total of four MAGA doves (including Stephen Miran).

That's still a minority. The total FOMC is 12, so two-thirds would not be MAGA doves. They would remain well short of an FOMC takeover. The President clearly wants Chair Powell to vacate his seat. If he does, it's still just one additional vote. Given that, the odds are that monetary policy does not get driven by the White House. Impacted, yes. But not dominated by.

But from a sentiment perspective, it's still a big deal. Attacking the Fed Chair like this is practically unprecedented. And in the extreme, dramatic curve steepening from both ends (with longer tenor yields up), and a weaker US dollar are likely consequences, should things really unravel. Still not our base view. But as a risk case view, it's rising in probability.

Dutch pension funds driving long end euro rates

Euro rates once again showed how domestic drivers can dominate global factors and Dutch pension funds are the ones moving markets this time. The second-largest Dutch pension fund (PFZW, €250bn AUM) published a more detailed outline of their new hedging strategy, which triggered a significant curve flattening on Monday.

In this updated plan, interest rate hedges will be 20% for younger participants and 100% for those in retirement. These ratios are likely significantly higher than markets anticipated. To put the numbers in perspective, ABP (the largest fund, aiming to transition in 2027) published hedging plans of 10% for younger people and 75% for elderly ones.

In effect, this means fewer fixed receiver swaps will have to be unwound, reducing the impact from the transition. We estimate that a 10 percentage point higher hedging ratio for PFZW translates to an increased demand for fixed receiver swaps of about €40m DV01.

Going forward, we expect more volatility from the Dutch pension fund transition. In the near term, we may see more flattening pressures as speculative positions get challenged. As pension flows start materialising, however, the overall net impact should still be steeper curves.

Tuesday's events and data view

The main release on Tuesday will be the US CPI. November's data was likely skewed by the late data collection given the government shutdown. Reverting to normal collection means our economists see some upside risk to the December figure, with a 0.4% month-on-month core rate that would lift the year-on-year figure to 2.8%.

Other data to watch are the ADP weekly data for the jobs market as well as the NFIB's small business sentiment indicator. Public comments by the Fed's Williams, Musalem and Barkin are likely to receive more scrutiny after the latest onslaught on Fed independence. Of note elsewhere are speeches from the UK's Governor Bailey and the ECB's Kocher.

Primary markets remain busy. The €SSA market kicked off the week with a €7bn, two-tranche deal from the EFSF (3y and 10y) and a €1bn 10y deal from Île-de-France. For Tuesday, the EU is lined up with a new 3y and 30y tap, while French CADES mandated a 7y social bond. While spreads are still tighter versus the end of last year, Monday showed signs of softness as spreads widened between 1bp to 2bp across the sectors.

In the govie space, Greece will be in the market for a new 10y benchmark. This comes alongside scheduled auctions from Austria (10y and 30y taps), Italy (new 3y) and Germany (new 5y). Elsewhere, the UK sells 10y gilt linkers and the US Treasury will auction US$22bn in 30y bonds.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Coca-Cola FEMSA: Reasonable Price, Solid Yield, And Venezuela Upside
data.description:
Coca-Cola FEMSA (KOF) is the world's largest independent Coca-Cola (KO) bottling franchise. It serves 78.7 million consumers in Mexico, along with approximately 200 million more consumers across the remainder of Latin America.

The company has long been part of Mexico's Fomento Economico Mexicano "FEMSA" (FMX) industrial conglomerate. FEMSA retains ownership of just under half of KOF's total outstanding stock and has a majority of the voting rights. This has been a beneficial relationship, as FEMSA operates its OXXO convenience store chain with more than 20,000 locations in Mexico and several thousand more around the rest of Latin America. This gives significant synergies in terms of branding and logistics for the distribution and sale of Coke-branded products within FEMSA's OXXO stores.

Both the parent FEMSA's shares and those of its Coca-Cola FEMSA subsidiary have delivered disappointing price performance over the past decade. However, these stocks have started to rally more recently amid a broader Latin American bull market. The Mexican stock market, as measured by the iShares MSCI Mexico ETF (EWW), has now nearly tripled since the March 2020 lows. That provides a solid tailwind for Mexican equity index components such as KOF stock.

Additionally, I believe the economies where the company operates, such as Mexico and Colombia, should benefit from broader economic developments such as the reshoring movement along with higher commodity prices. In addition, the recent developments in Venezuela present another potential upside catalyst.

Favorable Operating Results Trajectory

Coca-Cola FEMSA reported its Q3 results in October. They came in well ahead of expectations, with EPS of $1.52 easily topping the consensus estimate of $1.34. This continued a generally strong operational run for the company.

Though volumes were around flat for Q3, the company was able to grow operating income by nearly 7% thanks to increased prices and disciplined expense control. The Mexico market was weakest, with the company blaming a soft labor market and a drop in remittances as leading to slower consumer spending. However, this was offset by strength in other parts of LatAm. Brazil was a particular bright spot, as unit volumes increased nearly 3%, driven by market share gains.

Zooming out, analysts see the company's full-year 2025 revenues, as measured in dollars, jumping by 17.5%. A significant chunk of that has been driven by the appreciation of the Mexican peso and other LatAm currencies against the dollar over the past year. But organic growth is also adding to the momentum.

Analysts see revenues ticking along with 6% growth expected in 2026, and EPS in that year is projected to climb by 11% to $6.69 per share. That adds up to a perfectly reasonable 15x P/E multiple based on projected 2026 earnings.

While Coca-Cola FEMSA's share price remains far below its all-time highs set in the early 2010s, its EPS has significantly surpassed the prior peak and has shown a strong uptrend since 2021:

As a side note, as the chart shows, the company suffered a big loss at the time it deconsolidated its Venezuelan operations, and that speaks to potential upside if that asset becomes a significant contributor once again.

We can see the company's undemanding valuation on an EBITDA basis as well. Even after the recent rally, shares are still selling for just 8.3x EBITDA, which is well below the 15-year historical median:

In addition to the value proposition, there's also a strong income component as well.

Like many Latin American companies, Coca-Cola FEMSA does not have a fixed quarterly dividend payout. Rather, the dividend tends to fluctuate based on recent levels of profitability along with changes in exchange rates.

Regardless, Coca-Cola FEMSA has paid at least $2.47 per share in annual dividends in each of the past five years. That figure rose to nearly $4/share in 2025, and I'd expect it to rise again in 2026 as profitability has increased and the peso has appreciated against the dollar in recent months.

While Coca-Cola FEMSA's yield is variable, shares are usually good for at least a 3% annual payout, and it's been considerably higher than that over the past five years:

I'm not buying this stock just for, or even primarily for, the dividend. I think shares can head back to $150 each over the next two or three years. However, I do like getting a starting 3.9% yield as a kicker while the bullish thesis plays out.

Coca-Cola FEMSA: Venezuela's Coke Bottler

Coca-Cola FEMSA has long served as the Coca-Cola bottler for the nation of Venezuela. This used to be a significantly profitable part of the company's overall business back when the Venezuelan economy was performing better.

However, the Venezuelan economy ceased to function normally during the mid-2010s. Amid hyperinflation and sustained issues in obtaining inputs to manufacture products, foreign consumer packaged goods companies, including Coca-Cola FEMSA, have struggled to maintain even the semblance of normal operations.

A recent Seeking Alpha news article described that:

For its part, Coca-Cola is still present in Venezuela through a local bottler tied to Coca-Cola FEMSA, although the business has been deconsolidated and operates in a constrained, high-risk environment. Coca-Cola FEMSA holds an investment, referred to in the company's financial reporting as KOF Venezuela.

The U.S. campaign against President Nicolás Maduro and the increased U.S. involvement in Venezuela could set the stage for PepsiCo (PEP) and Coca-Cola to see enough improvement in supply chain management, plant modernization, and currency stabilization over the medium term to reset their strategies in the once key Latin American market.

What sort of impact could this have for Coca-Cola FEMSA and its investors in coming years?

In the company's 2024 integrated report, it noted that it currently serves about 276 million consumers across Mexico, Central America, and South America:

That figure, it appears, does not include Venezuela, which is currently treated as a non-consolidated operation.

As such, bringing Venezuela's roughly 30-35 million people back into the company's primary addressable market could possibly add more than 10% to topline revenues and earnings over the next few years if the Venezuelan economy reopens for business as usual.

That's not a huge figure on its own, but keep in mind that most consumer food and beverage companies have been struggling to show any meaningful growth at all in recent years. A potential low double-digit bump in revenues thanks to the renewed market opportunity in Venezuela could make KOF stock stand out from its food and beverage peers.

As a final note here, I'd highlight that Coca-Cola FEMSA has a broad and diversified geographic reach. I've seen some investors seem to think this is primarily just a Mexican company, and indeed it is headquartered there. But the company has an extensive footprint in Colombia, Brazil, and Guatemala, along with smaller presences in a number of other Latin American countries as well. As a result, I think people may be assigning too much Mexican political risk premium here; there's a lot of revenue coming in from other countries and currencies beyond Mexico.

KOF Stock Bottom Line

I've been eyeing KOF stock for several years, waiting for a potential catalyst that would make the stock break out of its decade-long slump:

The stock peaked back in 2013, at the height of the last Latin American boom. And, perhaps not coincidentally, the wheels fell off the Venezuelan economy right around then as well, taking away one of the company's larger markets at the time.

Now Latin American equities are in a new bull market, and Venezuela is potentially heading back toward a business-as-usual scenario. Both Colombia and Brazil, two significant Coca-Cola FEMSA markets, could see improved sentiment as well tied to their upcoming presidential elections later this year.

Meanwhile, KOF stock, which hasn't traded above $100/share since 2014, is now pushing right up against that resistance level. I think there's a good chance it breaks through the $100 barrier this time around. That makes me bullish on KOF stock for 2026. And in the bigger picture, I wouldn't be surprised to see 18-20x earnings on roughly $8/share of earnings power over the next few years, which argues for a longer-term price target around $150.

data.title: Traders Are Moving Past Powell's Investigation: U.S. Stock Index Outlook And Pre-CPI Trading Levels
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The morning session was nothing short of volatile. Overnight futures took a hit following news of the DoJ's investigation into Jerome Powell, once again casting a shadow over the Federal Reserve's independence. Yet, this market - and its traders - should never be underestimated.

Stocks caught a bid immediately after the open as participants shrugged off the headlines, using the early drop as yet another buying opportunity. Despite modest percentage gains on the day, the technical achievements are significant: the Dow is pacing for another record close, the Nasdaq is breaching monthly highs, and the S&P 500 is trading at fresh all-time highs.

Individual names are also making history, with Google (Alphabet) (GOOG) (GOOGL) recently breaking the $4T market cap milestone as Apple (AAPL) officially announced that they would require Google services for Apple Intelligence.

It seems "bad news is good news" in this environment. As long as the investigation remains a headline rather than an official indictment, investors remain resolutely bullish. Supplemented by heightened geopolitical pressures boosting defense and industrial sectors and driving metals to new highs, the trend remains intact.

However, expect momentum to potentially taper off as the day progresses; traders will likely begin squaring positions ahead of tomorrow's massive inflation (CPI) release at 8:30 AM (expected at +0.3% to 2.7% Y/Y)

Let's dive into our daily intra-session charts and NFP trading levels for the major US indexes: Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Healthcare is hurting once again, seeing quite some outflows in the beginning of this session after a huge 2025 trading. Financials are also struggling in the session as traders await key earnings for JPMorgan (JPM) and Bank of New York Mellon (BK) tomorrow, while energy drags from Exxon's (XOM) CEO comments on "uninvestable" Venezuela projects. The rest of the market shows green.

Dow Jones 2H Chart

Up a strong 1.16% since its open, the Dow is on pace to break new records again, now evolving within a strong intraday upward channel. A huge bull candle with no wicks is showing bull domination ahead of CPI, powered by its consumer defensive and machinery sectors - Walmart (WMT) is dominating the charts, up 3.70%.

  • A soft report tomorrow could easily send the Dow to 50,000.
  • A strong report, however, would hurt rate cut prospects and could lead to a retest of major support (the top candidate being 48,600 and 2H 200-MA).

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • 49,650 to 49,670 Current ATH Resistance
  • 46,670 All-Time Highs
  • 50,000 Potential Psychological Resistance

Support Levels

  • 49,324 2H 50-Period MA
  • 49,000 Psychological Pivot
  • 48,400 to 48,800 Major Support (2H MA 200 at 48,670)
  • Psychological Support at 48,000
  • 45,000 psychological level (main support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 2H Chart

After double top threats and sluggish price action throughout the past months, the Nasdaq is taking the lead again, brought up by the strong AI stock performance today:

Broadcom (AVGO), Oracle (ORCL), Google, a rebounding Nvidia (NVDA) and many others are pushing the tech heavy index to new highs, also breaking the double-top formed in the past week.

Still, traders will need to be cautious regarding short-timeframe bearish divergences. Tomorrow's NFP will be key to monitoring whether today's move fakes out or materializes into a real break.

  • Clearing the CPI, staying above 26,860 would point to a test of the October all-time highs.
  • Failing to stay above current resistance could lead to a test of the 25,000 level (25,100 is a key level to watch in this event; things would look bearish below).

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Intermediate resistance: 25,700 to 25,850 (breaking?)
  • Session highs: 25,877
  • All-time high resistance zone: 26,100 to 26,300
  • Current ATH: 26,283 (CFD)

Support Levels

  • 25,646 2H MA 50
  • Pivot 25,500 +/- 75 pts
  • Main Support: 25,000 to 25,250
  • 24,500 Main support
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 2H Chart

The S&P 500 went on to break its past day record highs yet again, with the new highs set at 6,986. Similarly to the Nasdaq, traders will need to be cautious regarding a so-so optimistic divergence forming as momentum slows during the ascent.

To mitigate the slow rise, however, a consistent uptrend sustains overall resilience in the market - the S&P could hold well a disappointing CPI tomorrow. Of course, it will depend on how bad it is (if it is).

A bullish CPI could well launch the S&P 500 beyond 7,000: 7,040 and 7,080 are potential profit-taking levels for the index.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • ATH Resistance: 6,945 to 6,975
  • Current All-Time High: 6,973
  • 1.382% Fib-Extension potential resistance at 7,001

Support Levels

  • 6,913 4H 50-MA Support
  • 6,800 Psychological Pivot and Range lows
  • Support 6,720 to 6,750 and 8H MA 50
  • 6,400 major psychological support

Safe trades and good luck for tomorrow's NFP!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: Another Blowout Earnings Look Inevitable
data.description:
It appears that buying Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) stock with a Strong Buy rating from me before the last earnings release was a good decision because the stock has delivered a total 9% return since then. The company releases its Q4 earnings this week (January 15th), and I think it is a good time to update coverage with the upcoming earnings preview.

I believe that TSM is poised to deliver another strong quarter because AI tailwinds are still strong with momentum accumulating. The company has a proven record of being able to capitalize on AI tailwinds, which is evident from strong revenue growth and margin expansion over the previous several quarters. Besides stellar fundamentals, there is a flawless earnings surprise record spanning the previous 12 quarters, accompanied by robust share price momentum. I am confident that TSM remains a Strong Buy into Q4 earnings.

TSM Q4 Earnings Preview

TSM has gained almost 51% in 2025, by far outperforming the S&P 500 Index (SPX). The stock's year-to-date performance in 2026 is also very confident, with a 6.5% share price increase compared to SPX's 1.8%. Overall, TSM's share price momentum is very strong with a 103% share price increase over the last nine months. TSM's robust share price momentum is also evident from the 14-day RSI indicator, currently sitting at 65. Such robust share price momentum is justified by TSM's rock-solid fundamentals.

As the world's largest semiconductor foundry, Taiwan Semiconductor is one of the major beneficiaries of the ongoing AI revolution. The company delivered 30%+ year-over-year revenue growth over the last six quarters, by far outpacing the last five years' 22.9% top line CAGR.

The crucial moment is that TSM is able to exercise strong pricing power due to soaring demand, as its key operating profitability ratios has increased aggressively. Over the same six quarters, the gross margin expanded by more than 600 basis points, while the operating margin increased by more than 800 basis points. Aggressive revenue growth combined with rapid profitability ratios growth confirms my assertion that TSM is extremely firmly positioned to capitalize on AI tailwinds.

For the upcoming quarter's earnings release, Wall Street analysts expect the trend of profitability outpacing revenue growth to continue. TSM's Q4 revenue is expected to increase by 22.9% year-over year, while the adjusted EPS is estimated to be up by 31.7%. TSM's record of double beats over the last twelve quarters suggests that the company is strong in delivering positive earnings surprises. Over the last sixteen quarters, there were zero EPS misses and only two negative revenue surprises. TSM's earnings surprise record over the last four years is almost flawless, which significantly increases the probability of another confident double beat on January 15.

Besides strong earnings surprise history, there are "more fundamental" reasons to believe that TSM's Q4 earnings release will be strong. The main reason is that AI tailwinds remain strong, which means more orders for TSM. Foxconn (FXCOF), another key manufacturer in the global AI supply chain, recently reported a strong 22% year-over-year revenue growth in Q4 2025. The company also reported that its December 2025 sales surged by 31.8% year-over-year, citing strong demand for AI-related electronics. TSM, itself, also recently reported strong 20.4% year-over-year revenue growth in December 2025.

Another evidence of AI revolution building its momentum is the recent presentation of Nvidia (NVDA) at CES 2026. Colette Kress, Nvidia's CFO, explicitly stated that Nvidia's previously cited $500 billion backlog through 2026 "has definitely gotten larger." She said that demand continues growing as customers are shifting toward agentic AI, which requires more computing power. Among potential demand drivers for NVDA in 2026, Colette Kress also mentioned China, as there is massive demand from this country's tech giants as well.

TSM is well positioned to further capitalize on AI tailwinds not only because of its strong recent historical performance but also because of its immense financial power. The company's tangible book value per share increased by almost 27% over the first three quarters of 2025. It is also vital to mention that TSM's net cash position increased significantly as well, from around $42 billion to $56.5 billion. Having such a strong financial position is a formidable asset for TSM because the industry is so demanding from the technological standpoint and the company needs to invest billions in R&D and new manufacturing facilities.

Valuation Analysis

Besides stellar fundamentals and highly likely blowout Q4 earnings, TSM is also attractively valued. With such strong AI tailwinds behind its back and TSM's status of the world's major semiconductor foundry, continued aggressive revenue and EPS growth looks inevitable. As a result, we see that TSM's estimated forward P/E ratio for FY2027 is expected to dip below 20. This is an extremely bullish setup, in my opinion.

TSM's compelling undervaluation is also confirmed by its extremely low non-GAAP PEG ratio of 1.23. As the table below suggests, it is almost 30% lower than the sector median. This is a massive gap, which does not look fair given TSM's aggressive revenue growth and rapidly expanding profitability.

Finally, Wall Street analysts also believe that TSM is notably undervalued. The average target price is $360, which indicates approximately 11% upside potential.

Mitigating Factors

One of the biggest headlines of 2026 are the military operation of the United States in Venezuela and large protests in Iran. While Venezuela and Iran are thousands of miles away from Taiwan, these developments increase the overall global geopolitical uncertainty. Moreover, we all know that there are historical tensions between Taiwan and China. I am not sure whether China will ever conduct a military operation against Taiwan because of the latter's historical political ties with the U.S. However, China held military drills around Taiwan just recently, which might mean they are preparing for future escalation. I think that probability of such a scenario is very low, but the potential adverse magnitude will be huge if the risk materializes.

If we speak about the upcoming earnings release itself, I believe that the key risk is the market's unpredictable reaction. I have a high level of conviction that TSM will deliver a strong quarter with an optimistic outlook. However, it might not be sufficient to impress the market because expectations around AI revolution are already quite aggressive. For example, Nvidia's latest earnings release as of November 19th was stellar, with many bullish forward-looking comments from CEO Jensen Huang. However, Nvidia's share price has been stagnating since then. Therefore, there might be muted reaction even to stellar earnings.

Conclusion

I continue buying TSM before the upcoming earnings release because I am confident that the company will deliver strong Q4 performance with the management's positive outlook for FY2026. Valuation remains compelling, meaning that TSM is a Strong Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
